The European Union Policy and Normative 

Framework in Addressing Climate Mobility * 

by Samantha Velluti  

Associate Professor in European and Constitutional Law  

University of Sussex 

Abstract [En]: This contribution examines the role of the EU in addressing the multi-faceted challenges posed 
by climate mobility. Specifically, it looks at the way the EU addresses the manifold issues of climate mobility and 
the extent to which it has developed effective, just and long-term solutions for climate migrants. It is argued that 
a more holistic approach is needed, increasing and better coordinating its multifarious actions on climate mobility, 
through stronger institutional and policy coherence. At the same time, in order to adequately protect what is a 
particularly vulnerable class of migrants, it is argued that EU migration rules should depart from narratives of mass 
migration and migration crisis, which largely focus on migration preparedness and management. 
 
Titolo: Il quadro politico e normativo dell’Unione Europea per affrontare la mobilita’ climatica  
Abstract [It]: Il presente contributo esamina il ruolo della UE nell’affrontare le molteplici sfide poste dalla 
migrazione climatica. Nello specifico, l’analisi focalizza sul modo in cui la UE affronta le varie questioni della 
migrazione climatica e la misura in cui ha sviluppato soluzioni efficaci, giuste e a lungo termine per i migranti 
climatici. Si sostiene che sia necessario un approccio più olistico, aumentando e coordinando meglio le variegate 
misure sulla mobilità climatica della UE, attraverso una maggiore coerenza istituzionale e politica. Allo stesso 
tempo, al fine di proteggere adeguatamente quella che è una categoria di migranti particolarmente vulnerabile, si 
rende altresì necessario riscattare le politiche migratorie della UE da narrative sulla migrazione concepita come 
movimento di massa e come crisi migratoria, di modo che tali politiche cessino di concentrarsi in larga parte su 
misure di prevenzione e di gestione della migrazione.  
   
Keywords: EU, climate change, Global climate governance, climate mobility, migration, forced displacement, 1951 
Refugee Convention, complementary protection, EU Qualification Regime 
Parole chiave: UE, cambiamento climatico, governance globale del clima, migrazione climatica, flussi migratori, 
spostamento forzato, Convenzione di Ginevra del 1951, protezione complementare, il regime qualifiche UE 
 
Table of Contents: 1. Introduction. 2. The United Nations Climate Regime and the Role of the European Union 
in Global Climate Governance. 3. The Challenges posed by the Climate Change-Migration Nexus. 4. The 
European Union’s response to climate mobility. 5. The EU Qualification regime as a form of complementary 
protection. 6. Conclusion. 
 

1. Introduction 

Climate change is altering the character of human mobility in myriad ways: migration is taking more 

complex forms, altering traditional ones that are based on more linear and clearly distinguishable causal 

narratives1. Responses to climate migration have been fragmented and fraught to date, owing partly to 

the contentious nature of climate change and migration and partly to the multivarious stakeholders,

interests and jurisdictions involved2. Consequently, and precisely because of the wide range of mobility 

outcomes, the distinction between certain protection norms or frameworks is excessively rigid and 

outdated. What is required is a two-pronged legal and policy approach that seeks to minimise forced 

displacement while facilitating, where necessary, migration as an adaptation strategy to ensure that all 

those affected by climate change receive appropriate support. Adequate protection of climate migrants3 

with sound planning for and management of climate mobility has become critical for human security. 

What is required, therefore, is a more holistic approach with more concerted efforts and greater 

international responsibility-sharing to support host communities develop effective localised practices 

world-wide. Countries concerned need to better ensure that climate change adaptation measures, both 

incremental and transformational, address climate-driven human mobility. 

The European Union (EU) has been steadily positioning itself as a key international player in the attempt 

to improve global governance, through increased cooperation with other international organisations4. 

Since its inception the EU, has developed ‘a complex web of relations founded on and operating within 

international law, and these relations mediate between EU law and third countries and international 

organisations’5. Moreover, the respect for the rule of law, protection of human rights, and the strict 

observance and development of international law have been included among the EU action’s overall 

objectives and mission statement6. The EU has set itself a path towards having a significant role globally 

in relation to normative objectives of global justice. According to its mandate the EU is ‘morally, 

politically and legally held to pursue equality amongst wealthy and poorer nations, to support their

empirical foundation for the causal link between climate change and migration explains the absence of a clear definition 
of “climate migrant” and existing disagreements among academics and policy-makers as to a notion that can 
comprehensively capture the complexity of this type of human mobility. The debate is centred around two main and 
opposing schools of thought, namely maximalism and minimalism. The former conceives of the link between 
environmental change and migration as causative and direct. As such it predicts large scale human mobility as a necessary

The EU’s constitutional, normative and institutional set up, including its array of competences, 

‘cushioned by human rights demonstrate that the EU can play a significant role in developing preventative 

or proactive protection measures and serve as a key partner in building long-term or reactive protection 

measures for climate migrants. 8’ Europe also has an advanced regional protection regime embracing and 

building on a variety of asylum, refugee and human rights law and principles and can guarantee greater 

uniformity in the law and practice of its member states9. Additionally, the EU has been tackling climate 

change and ecological degradation for decades as ‘a self-proclaimed pro-environmental and human rights-

oriented global actor. 10’  

This contribution examines the role of the EU in addressing the multi-faceted challenges posed by climate 

mobility. Specifically, it looks at the way the EU addresses the manifold issues of climate mobility and to 

what extent it has developed effective and just long-term solutions for environmentally displaced persons 

and climate migrants. The paper starts by providing an overview of international climate policy and the 

role of the EU within it. It then examines the challenges posed by the climate change-migration nexus, 

which provides the basis for the analysis of the EU response to climate mobility, including an evaluation 

of the EU Qualification regime as a form of complementary protection. The conclusion brings together 

the main points of the paper. 

The central argument is that a more holistic approach is needed, increasing and better coordinating its 

multifarious actions on climate mobility, through stronger institutional and policy coherence. At the same 

time, in order to adequately protect what is a particularly vulnerable class of migrants, it should depart 

from narratives of mass migration and migration crisis, which largely focus on migration preparedness 

and management.

2. The United Nations Climate Regime and the Role of the European Union in Global Climate 

Governance  

The EU has become a key international player in the fight against climate change, particularly in the 

context of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) annual 

Conference of the Parties (COP) meetings, 11 which serve the purpose of reviewing the implementation 

of climate frameworks and assess the need for adopting further instruments12.  

The 1997 Kyoto Protocol complements the UNFCCC by operationalising it13. The treaty commits state 

parties (both developed and developing states) to curb greenhouse gas emissions14. Of note are also the 

Cancun Agreements15, which include the 2010 Cancun Adaptation Framework aimed at strengthening 

international cooperation and ensuring coherent approaches to climate adaptation. In particular, at the 

2010 COP16 parties to the UNFCCC recognised for the first time the need for ‘measures to enhance 

understanding, coordination and cooperation with regard to climate-induced displacement, migration and 

planned relocation, where appropriate, at the national, regional and international levels. 16’ This provision 

proved to be crucial in prompting and spawning subsequent policy developments on climate mobility, 

particularly through the state-led multi-stakeholder Nansen Initiative, which was launched by the 

governments of Norway and Switzerland17. However, on a more critical note reference to climate 

migration was associated with an invitation to all parties to the UNFCCC to enhance actions on 

adaptation, rather than requiring them to take such actions18. In addition, the provision did not single out 

developed countries for more responsibility, as proposed by many developing countries19.  

Despite the soft nature of these first developments, the afore-mentioned Nansen Initiative constituted 

an important milestone in the recognition and protection of climate migrants as it aimed at filling the 

legal protection gap by building a consensus among states to endorse the adoption and implementation 

of a non-legally binding agenda for the protection of cross-border displaced persons in the context of

with at least 10 out of 17 goals containing targets and indicators that are directly relevant to migration or

The 2020 European Green Deal has to be understood in the context of these international climate 

governance measures and initiatives28. It comprises a set of initiatives by the European Commission to 

transform EU policies on climate, energy, transport and taxation to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions 

by at least 55% by 2030 compared to levels of 1990, with the overarching aim of achieving a climate-

neutral Europe by 2050. In its Communication on a European Green Deal initiative, the EU Commission 

acknowledged the impact of climate change on population displacement and forced migration and the 

need to adopt resilience measures as a form of prevention29.  

Overall, the package includes:  

- 
emission reduction targets across a wide range of sectors;  

- 
a goal of increasing natural carbon sinks;  

- 
an updated emissions trading system aimed at limiting emissions, putting a price on pollution and 

generating investment in the green transition; and,  

- 
social support for citizens and small businesses, particularly with the setting up of a Just 

Transition Mechanism, and the adoption of an Investment Plan and a Circular Economy Action 

Plan.  

The EU now has legally binding climate objectives that cover all key sectors of the economy with the 

2021 European Climate Law translating into law the goal set out in the European Green Deal30.  

 

3. The Challenges posed by the Climate Change-Migration Nexus  

Climate migration stems from the interplay between two conceptually complex and politically 

contentious phenomena: climate change and migration31. Climate change poses questions that are at the 

core of social and environmental developments of the 21st century, such as environmental and climate 

justice as well as Global North–Global South relations. Debates on the multi-faceted impact of climate 

change on migration date back to the 1980s. Nevertheless, the problems of establishing causality that are 

associated with the ways in which environmental events and environmental changes - namely “slow-onset

environmental changes” and “rapid-onset changes”- interact with human mobility, and the identification 

of viable long-term solutions for distinct environmental changes and effects on displacement and 

migratory movements 32 remain a moot and divisive point.  

Climate-induced migration needs to be addressed with viable long-term and large-scale solutions, 

particularly from the perspective of legal protection.  At present there is a clear legal protection gap with 

regard to climate-induced refugees who are neither defined as a legal category in their own right nor 

covered by the 1951 Refugee Convention33. In a narrow sense, the definition of “refugee” under Article 

1A of the Convention does not apply to people who are displaced for environment-related reasons 

because “environmental degradation” does not fall within the meaning of “persecution” of the Refugee 

Convention34. Relatedly, there would be the additional hurdle of linking such form of persecution to one 

of the grounds set out in the Convention35. For these reasons, international refugee lawyers tend to 

consider environmentally induced displacement to fall outside the scope of the 1951 Refugee Convention 

and its additional Protocol36. That said, the relationship between human rights and climate change is 

increasingly recognized in law as illustrated by the UN Human Rights Committee’s Teitiota decision37. 

This was the first decision on a complaint from an individual seeking asylum from the effects of climate 

change.

The answer to overcome these legal hurdles has been largely in the form of soft law instruments. The 

2016 UN New York Declaration for Refugees and Migrants 38 recognises climate change, natural disasters 

and other environmental factors as drivers of migration and commits to addressing drivers of mass 

movements. As part of this effort, the document sets out a plan for comprehensive refugee response 

frameworks as well as two global compacts – one on refugees and one on international migration. In 

particular, the 2018 Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration (GCM) 39 – the first ever 

UN global agreement that sets out common pledges to address challenges related to international 

migration- warrants attention. While the GCM is non-binding in nature it marks an important milestone 

in identifying climate change and other environmental factors as drivers of migration and in outlining 

areas of cooperation to address the issue. As such, the GCM formulates a comprehensive understanding 

of the challenges linked to the connections between climate change, the environment and migration and 

builds on various international frameworks. The GCM acknowledges the multi-causality of migration as 

environmental drivers interact with political, economic and demographic drivers. Significantly, the GCM 

outlines the importance of working at the regional and state levels to address environmental drivers of 

migration, promoting cooperation with neighbouring and other relevant countries, as well as the need to 

harmonize and develop approaches and mechanisms also at the subregional levels to tackle the 

vulnerabilities of persons affected by sudden-onset and slow-onset environmental drivers. 

 

4. The European Union’s response to climate mobility 

In the EU context climate mobility and, in particular, the nexus between climate change and migration 

gained more prominence at the end of the 1990s. Institutionally, the European Parliament (EP) has been 

at the forefront in advocating the adoption of EU measures on climate mobility. In its 1999 Resolution 

it explicitly acknowledged and proposed that the category of environmental refugees be given official 

recognition and addressed the links between environmental degradation and security concerns in 

international relations40. In its 2016 Resolution, the EP specifically addressed environmental degradation 

as one of the triggers of population movements41. It called for the EU to adopt a long-term strategy to 

tackle the “push factors” in developing countries, citing climate change and natural disasters as examples

Consequently, the EU as a whole is unable to (or is reluctant to) embrace a fully-fledged human rights 

and climate justice-based approach that adequately considers and speaks to the specific needs, agencies 

and vulnerabilities of climate migrants and displaced people. The Commission’s 2022 SWD 63 while

of migration and as one of several root causes of irregular migration and forced displacement64. Even 

though it mentions legal migration pathways to Europe, it does not say how these can be developed in 

relation to climate change displacement and migration.  

 

5. The EU Qualification regime as a form of complementary protection  

Since climate change is a “threat multiplier” - that is it is multicausal and multifactorial rather than one 

of the recognized grounds for refugee status under the 1951 Refugee Convention- there is no category 

of “climate refugee” as a matter of law65. International refugee law is inappropriate to fulfill the needs 

that result from climate mobility and it is thus of limited application. In this regard, it has been observed 

that in cases of slow-onset environmental degradation, people seek support for alternative forms of 

livelihood and earning a living rather than seeking refugee-type protection66. Moreover, because of its 

long-term nature, it would equally not be appropriate for rapid-onset climate events, which cause 

temporary (and often internal) displacement67.

Our attention, therefore, necessarily needs to turn to complementary protection68. It is not a term of art 

as it is not defined in any international instrument. Complementary protection allows states to provide 

protection from removal on human rights grounds through the principle of non-refoulement for individuals 

who fall outside the scope of the 1951 Convention and its 1967 Protocol. International human rights law 

may constitute a basis for complementary protection claims of climate migrants. While state practices 

and mechanisms affording complementary protection will vary as to the nature and level of protection 

and the eligibility criteria and legal statuses, they derive largely from International human rights 

instruments such as the 1966 International Covenant for Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) 69 and the 

Convention against Torture (CAT). In particular, climate change will affect the right to life 70 and the 

prohibition of torture and inhuman and degrading treatment71, as well as other rights such as the right to 

adequate food72, the right to water73, the right to health74, the right to property 75 and the right to adequate 

housing76. 

At EU level, an example of complementary protection is subsidiary protection under the Qualification 

Directive, (QD) 77, which was updated and replaced by the Qualification Regulation (QR) 78. This EU 

legislative instrument has been defined as being the first supranational codification of a complementary 

protection regime 79 and for this reason it has been at the centre of much academic discussion as to 

whether it can provide a valid alternative for climate migrants80. Individuals not qualifying for refugee

substantial grounds have been shown for believing that they would face a real risk of serious harm if

The initial draft of the QD included among the sources of serious harm an explicit reference to ‘a threat 

to his or her life, safety or freedom as a result of systematic or generalised violations of their human 

rights’81. The provision was wide-ranging to the point that it could also extend to human rights violations 

without conflict and thus also potentially cover climate migrants 82. However, on the notion and sources 

of serious harm the final version of Article 15 QD only referred to situations of conflict. Moreover, 

Article 8 QD provided that an individual was not in need of international protection if in a part of the 

country of origin, he or she had no well-founded fear of being persecuted or was not at real risk of 

suffering serious harm; or had access to protection against persecution or serious harm; and he or she 

could safely and legally travel to and gain admittance to that part of the country and could reasonably be 

expected to settle there. So long as such individual could move to another part of their country and/or 

have access to protection following an adverse climate change event, then it remained unlikely that such 

person would be granted subsidiary protection.  

Article 15 QD also referred to death penalty and execution (a) or torture or inhuman or degrading 

treatment or punishment of an applicant in the country of origin (b). While the former source of serious 

harm is of no relevance to climate migrants, the latter source could have some limited relevance in relation 

to environmental degradation and disaster-related harm, in line with the jurisprudence of the European 

Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) and the EU Court of Justice (CJEU). An interpretation of ‘torture or 

inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment’ in accordance with Article 3 ECHR and Article 4 EU 

Charter of Fundamental Rights could provide the basis for enabling the EU to address climate migration. 

A series of judicial decisions in Austria show that Article 3 ECHR was taken into account in the granting 

of subsidiary protection under the QD83. Significantly, no “human actor” of serious harm in the country 

of origin was necessary as required by the jurisprudence of the CJEU84. That said, and putting aside the

though the EP had proposed the inclusion of, among others, ‘internal conflicts, severe violations of 

human rights or events seriously disturbing public order in the country of origin, or in a part thereof’ as 

factors to be taken into account when determining whether indiscriminate violence exists100. The proposal 

received insufficient political support within the EP itself and, therefore, was not included in its mandate 

for negotiations with the Council. This suggests that in the context of the Common European Asylum 

System (CEAS) the securitisation and deterrence paradigms remain underlying elements of the EU’s 

approach to migration to the detriment of its protection agenda.

6. Conclusion  

Climate change was once considered an environmental problem, but it now impinges on every aspect of 

human life 101 including migration. For this reason, the nexus between climate change and human mobility 

is highly complex both in terms of its conceptualisation, governance and adequate protection standards 

for environmentally displaced persons and climate migrants. While the connection between climate 

change-related disasters and human mobility has been acknowledged at EU level for some time, the policy 

and legal framework of the EU remains fragmented. As to complementary protection, the examination 

of EU asylum law and, in particular, the EU Qualification Directive and the relevant case law, 

demonstrated that the conceptual constraints embedded in its design and largely confirmed in the 

European Courts’ jurisprudence stifle protection. This is particularly the case when the harm is 

generalized - as is often the case with climate mobility- and does not meet the high threshold set out by 

the European Courts in relation to “serious harm” as provided in Article 15(b) QD. These limitations 

are particularly problematic for those affected by the effects of slow-onset climate change ‘where people 

know that their human rights will be adversely impacted but do not know when this will happen.102’  

More steps need to be taken by the EU to develop a “European Model” of international protection for 

climate migrants and environmentally displaced persons, rooted in human rights and international climate 

change law, as opposed to deterrence and state-driven security considerations. The EU could draw 

inspiration from national legislation which has circumvented the impasse of the status of refugee and the 

limitations of subsidiary protection by focusing on humanitarian protection and by adding an explicit 

reference to environmental catastrophe103. There is no one-size-fits-all solution, particularly given the 

conceptual complexity of climate migration and the composite legal landscape of EU asylum and 

migration law. Nevertheless, with an increasing changing climate it is a challenge that the EU can no 

longer put on hold. Moreover, as Hush maintains ‘uniform EU policy for environmentally-displaced
